Authors: Max Hellers; Jonas Tusch; Carina Gerritzen; Eric Hasenstab-Dübler; Mario Fischer-Gödde; Andreas R.A. Schneider; Chris S. Marien; Josua J. Pakulla; R. Hugh Smithies; Martin J. Van Kranendonk; Dieter Garbe-Schönberg; Carsten Münker

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120056

Relevance: 0.577 (consider)

The Narryer Terrane is known for its Hadean zircons, demonstrating the existence of evolved Hadean crust in the Yilgarn Craton (W-Australia). However, the effects of Hadean material on long- and short-lived isotope signatures have remained ambiguous. We present the first combined 146 Sm- 142 Nd and 182 Hf- 182 W dataset for rocks from the Yilgarn Craton to identify putative Hadean components in crustal and mantle reservoirs through time. We investigated these short-lived isotope systems in mafic-ultramafic sequences, metasediments and granitoids covering the oldest preserved Paleoarchean rocks up to the Neoarchean granitoids that mark the stabilisation of the craton. Our study reveals modern mantle-like 142 Nd/ 144 Nd in Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and Neoarchean komatiites, suggesting crustal and mantle sources that, at the time of extraction, had already homogenised isotope anomalies from Hadean Sm/Nd silicate fractionation events. We propose that slight µ 142 Nd variations between Williamstown Peridotite, Paringa Basalt, and Kambalda Komatiite samples from the Kalgoorlie Terrane resulted from assimilation of an ancient crustal component. Unlike 142 Nd patterns, many Yilgarn samples show clearly resolvable excesses in µ 182 W that range from +7.5 ± 3.0 to +10.3 ± 2.8 for Paleo- to Neoarchean granitoids and µ 182 W = +4.1 ± 0.8 (95% c.i., n = 11) for Neoarchean mafic-ultramafic sequences. The 182 W excesses likely record isotope signatures inherited from core-mantle differentiation and insufficient inmixing of late veneer material, preserved in the different mantle and crustal reservoirs related to the Yilgarn Craton. Overall, our results demonstrate that the Paleo- to Neoarchean mantle and crustal magma sources had been isotopically homogenised, with limited signatures from Hadean silicate differentiation but had not incorporated the complete late veneer budget of the modern mantle.

item.title: Glacial dysoxia in the deep subpolar North Atlantic during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition
item.dc:creator: Iván Hernández-Almeida
Francisco Javier Sierro
Gabriel M. Filippelli
Antje H. L. Voelker
Paula Diz
item.description: Abstract The transition from 41-kyr to 100-kyr climate cycles during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT; 1250–700 kyr), occurred in the absence of any significant shift in orbital forcing. The increase of carbon storage in the deep ocean between Marine Isotope Stage (MIS) 24–22 has been suggested as a main internal factor leading to the transition to 100-kyr glacial cycles. We present sedimentary redox proxies and benthic foraminifera assemblages that demonstrate persistent dysoxic conditions during the MPT at IODP Site U1314 (subpolar North Atlantic). During glacials between 940 and 870 kyr, benthic foraminifera species typical of high porewater oxygen concentration disappeared, and concentrations of manganese oxides and reactive phosphorus mineral phases, both influenced by redox state, showed reductions exceeding 50%. Here, we show that higher freshwater supply associated with ice-rafted delivery caused a reduction in deep-water convection, decreasing bottom-water oxygenation and favoring carbon storage in the subpolar North Atlantic during the MPT. Published: 2026-04-24T09:03:03+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Iván Hernández-Almeida; Francisco Javier Sierro; Gabriel M. Filippelli; Antje H. L. Voelker; Paula Diz

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-71268-4

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Abstract The transition from 41-kyr to 100-kyr climate cycles during the Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT; 1250–700 kyr), occurred in the absence of any significant shift in orbital forcing. The increase of carbon storage in the deep ocean between Marine Isotope Stage (MIS) 24–22 has been suggested as a main internal factor leading to the transition to 100-kyr glacial cycles. We present sedimentary redox proxies and benthic foraminifera assemblages that demonstrate persistent dysoxic conditions during the MPT at IODP Site U1314 (subpolar North Atlantic). During glacials between 940 and 870 kyr, benthic foraminifera species typical of high porewater oxygen concentration disappeared, and concentrations of manganese oxides and reactive phosphorus mineral phases, both influenced by redox state, showed reductions exceeding 50%. Here, we show that higher freshwater supply associated with ice-rafted delivery caused a reduction in deep-water convection, decreasing bottom-water oxygenation and favoring carbon storage in the subpolar North Atlantic during the MPT.

item.title: Petrogenesis of pegmatites in the Shakhdara−Alichur dome, South Pamir (Tajikistan): Constraints from apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis
item.dc:creator: Murodov Azamdzhon
Amjad Hussain
Sharifjon Odinaev
Davlatkhudzha Murodov
Sherzod Abdulov
Amirkhamza Murodov
Xin Ma
Ilhomjon Oimuhammadzoda
Safaralizoda Nosir Saidjalol
Wei Dan
Gong-Jian Tang
item.description: The petrogenesis of pegmatites in collisional orogens provides key insights into crustal melting and reworking. To investigate the age and source of pegmatites in the Shakhdara gneiss dome (South Pamir, Tajikistan), we integrated in situ apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, trace-element geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis. U-Pb ages reveal pegmatite crystallization in the late Miocene (ca. 14−11 Ma for apatite; ca. 21−10 Ma for monazite) and host gneiss formation in the Eocene−Oligocene (ca. 35−27 Ma for apatite; ca. 28−22 Ma for monazite). The Nd isotopes show that pegmatite apatite [εNd(t) = −32.2 to +3.0] preserves signatures of reworked ancient crust, while monazite in both lithologies [εNd(t) = −20.8 to −4.2] reflects Nd equilibration during high-temperature anatexis. The F-rich, heavy rare earth element (HREE)-enriched, and Sr-depleted signature of the pegmatite apatite indicates crystallization from an evolved felsic melt. This melt was generated via anatexis of ancient basement crust, triggered by asthenospheric heating during lithospheric extension. In contrast, gneiss apatite is Cl-rich and light REE (LREE)-enriched, consistent with metamorphic recrystallization. Similarly, gneiss monazites are homogeneous and LREE-rich, contrasting with the chemically variable pegmatite monazites. Texturally, pegmatite minerals preserve euhedral forms, whereas gneiss grains show embayed margins and patchy zoning indicative of fluid-assisted recrystallization. Collectively, these features define a two-stage, genetically linked model for REE enrichment. Stage A (ca. 12.9−11.0 Ma) involved magmatic differentiation, which enriched the pegmatites in REEs and homogenized the isotopic signatures of the host gneiss. Stage B (ca. 11−10 Ma) featured hydrothermal overprinting by resolved fluids, driving the dissolution−reprecipitation of apatite and monazite. This magmatic−hydrothermal continuum confirms a common anatectic source. While apatite records signatures from ancient sources and fluids, monazite reflects isotopic homogenization, documenting the multistage reworking of the crust of an evolving collisional orogen. This study provides critical constraints on the petrogenesis and mineralization potential of pegmatite−gneiss systems in collisional orogens worldwide. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:36+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Murodov Azamdzhon; Amjad Hussain; Sharifjon Odinaev; Davlatkhudzha Murodov; Sherzod Abdulov; Amirkhamza Murodov; Xin Ma; Ilhomjon Oimuhammadzoda; Safaralizoda Nosir Saidjalol; Wei Dan; Gong-Jian Tang

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b39007.1

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

The petrogenesis of pegmatites in collisional orogens provides key insights into crustal melting and reworking. To investigate the age and source of pegmatites in the Shakhdara gneiss dome (South Pamir, Tajikistan), we integrated in situ apatite and monazite U-Pb geochronology, trace-element geochemistry, and Sm-Nd isotope analysis. U-Pb ages reveal pegmatite crystallization in the late Miocene (ca. 14−11 Ma for apatite; ca. 21−10 Ma for monazite) and host gneiss formation in the Eocene−Oligocene (ca. 35−27 Ma for apatite; ca. 28−22 Ma for monazite). The Nd isotopes show that pegmatite apatite [εNd(t) = −32.2 to +3.0] preserves signatures of reworked ancient crust, while monazite in both lithologies [εNd(t) = −20.8 to −4.2] reflects Nd equilibration during high-temperature anatexis. The F-rich, heavy rare earth element (HREE)-enriched, and Sr-depleted signature of the pegmatite apatite indicates crystallization from an evolved felsic melt. This melt was generated via anatexis of ancient basement crust, triggered by asthenospheric heating during lithospheric extension. In contrast, gneiss apatite is Cl-rich and light REE (LREE)-enriched, consistent with metamorphic recrystallization. Similarly, gneiss monazites are homogeneous and LREE-rich, contrasting with the chemically variable pegmatite monazites. Texturally, pegmatite minerals preserve euhedral forms, whereas gneiss grains show embayed margins and patchy zoning indicative of fluid-assisted recrystallization. Collectively, these features define a two-stage, genetically linked model for REE enrichment. Stage A (ca. 12.9−11.0 Ma) involved magmatic differentiation, which enriched the pegmatites in REEs and homogenized the isotopic signatures of the host gneiss. Stage B (ca. 11−10 Ma) featured hydrothermal overprinting by resolved fluids, driving the dissolution−reprecipitation of apatite and monazite. This magmatic−hydrothermal continuum confirms a common anatectic source. While apatite records signatures from ancient sources and fluids, monazite reflects isotopic homogenization, documenting the multistage reworking of the crust of an evolving collisional orogen. This study provides critical constraints on the petrogenesis and mineralization potential of pegmatite−gneiss systems in collisional orogens worldwide.

item.title: Deciphering grain-size reduction as a driver of mid-lithosphere discontinuity formation
item.dc:creator: Mingqi Liu
Taras V. Gerya
Zhong-Hai Li
Ling Chen
James A. D. Connolly
item.description: The mid-lithosphere discontinuity (MLD) is a fundamental yet enigmatic seismic boundary within Earth’s lithosphere, whose origin remains debated despite decades of study. Using thermomechanical models with self-consistent grain-size evolution, we propose a previously unrecognized mechanism for MLD formation. Plate deformation induces pronounced grain-size reduction within the brittle-ductile transition, generating permeability barriers that trap ascending fluids. These fluid-rich layers reproduce the observed seismic velocity drops. Their depth scales with lithospheric thickness and thermal structure and is fine-tuned by rheology evolution through brittle-ductile partitioning. The interplay between grain-size reduction and fluid accumulation imparts dual mechanical behavior to the MLD, stabilizing it within cold, ancient lithosphere or enabling localized failure under thermal perturbation. This framework identifies grain-scale processes as the primary control on MLD formation and links mantle-lithosphere coupling to planetary lithospheric evolution. Published: 2026-04-24T17:58:32+00:00 Venue: Science Advances Score: 0.568 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mingqi Liu; Taras V. Gerya; Zhong-Hai Li; Ling Chen; James A. D. Connolly

Journal: Science Advances

DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.aed4229

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The mid-lithosphere discontinuity (MLD) is a fundamental yet enigmatic seismic boundary within Earth’s lithosphere, whose origin remains debated despite decades of study. Using thermomechanical models with self-consistent grain-size evolution, we propose a previously unrecognized mechanism for MLD formation. Plate deformation induces pronounced grain-size reduction within the brittle-ductile transition, generating permeability barriers that trap ascending fluids. These fluid-rich layers reproduce the observed seismic velocity drops. Their depth scales with lithospheric thickness and thermal structure and is fine-tuned by rheology evolution through brittle-ductile partitioning. The interplay between grain-size reduction and fluid accumulation imparts dual mechanical behavior to the MLD, stabilizing it within cold, ancient lithosphere or enabling localized failure under thermal perturbation. This framework identifies grain-scale processes as the primary control on MLD formation and links mantle-lithosphere coupling to planetary lithospheric evolution.

item.title: Polycyclic Metamorphism, Exhumation, and Recycling of Subduction Complex Rocks, Cedros Island, Baja California
item.dc:creator: Jordan W. Wang
Paul Kapp
Robert Holder
John He
David Hernández‐Uribe
James Worthington
item.description: Abstract High‐pressure rocks from subduction complexes are key records of the physical and chemical processes that operate on the subduction interface, but interpretation of these records requires accurate structural understanding of where they formed in the subduction zone and the mechanisms by which they were exhumed. We present new geologic mapping, outcrop‐scale observations, and geochronology from subduction‐zone assemblages at Punta Prieta Ridge on Cedros Island, Baja California (Mexico), to investigate the history of subduction, exhumation, and structural assembly of these rocks. The rocks of Punta Prieta Ridge are exposed in the footwalls of high‐ and low‐angle normal faults that carry Cretaceous forearc basin strata and attenuated mantle sections of the Cedros Island Ophiolite in their hangingwalls. The footwall rocks are subduction‐zone assemblages organized into distinct nappes that decrease in metamorphic grade and degree of strain structurally downward. Garnet‐amphibolite and blueschist blocks within the subduction complex yield 40 Ar/ 39 Ar cooling ages between 172 and 144 Ma and are hosted in siliciclastic rocks which yield detrital zircon maximum depositional ages between 92 and 72 Ma. Based on field evidence and age relationships, we interpret the occurrence of older higher‐grade blocks in younger clastic matrix to be depositional rather than the result of tectonic mixing. We then present a model for their exhumation and assembly via multiple cycles of extensional unroofing of the subduction complex and sedimentary recycling of high‐pressure rocks back into the subduction trench. We conclude by comparing the record of subduction on Cedros Island to other parts of the Cordillera. Published: 2026-04-24T06:45:13+00:00 Venue: Tectonics Score: 0.563 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jordan W. Wang; Paul Kapp; Robert Holder; John He; David Hernández‐Uribe; James Worthington

Journal: Tectonics

DOI: 10.1029/2025tc009340

Relevance: 0.563 (consider)

Abstract High‐pressure rocks from subduction complexes are key records of the physical and chemical processes that operate on the subduction interface, but interpretation of these records requires accurate structural understanding of where they formed in the subduction zone and the mechanisms by which they were exhumed. We present new geologic mapping, outcrop‐scale observations, and geochronology from subduction‐zone assemblages at Punta Prieta Ridge on Cedros Island, Baja California (Mexico), to investigate the history of subduction, exhumation, and structural assembly of these rocks. The rocks of Punta Prieta Ridge are exposed in the footwalls of high‐ and low‐angle normal faults that carry Cretaceous forearc basin strata and attenuated mantle sections of the Cedros Island Ophiolite in their hangingwalls. The footwall rocks are subduction‐zone assemblages organized into distinct nappes that decrease in metamorphic grade and degree of strain structurally downward. Garnet‐amphibolite and blueschist blocks within the subduction complex yield 40 Ar/ 39 Ar cooling ages between 172 and 144 Ma and are hosted in siliciclastic rocks which yield detrital zircon maximum depositional ages between 92 and 72 Ma. Based on field evidence and age relationships, we interpret the occurrence of older higher‐grade blocks in younger clastic matrix to be depositional rather than the result of tectonic mixing. We then present a model for their exhumation and assembly via multiple cycles of extensional unroofing of the subduction complex and sedimentary recycling of high‐pressure rocks back into the subduction trench. We conclude by comparing the record of subduction on Cedros Island to other parts of the Cordillera.

item.title: Long-lived (&gt;20 m.y.) prograde heating in the Eastern Himalaya in response to Neo-Tethys slab breakoff
item.dc:creator: Shuaiqi Liu
Guibin Zhang
Simin Huang
Mingcheng Duan
Lu Xiong
Shuzhen Wang
item.description: The Himalaya is the most prominent collisional belt on Earth, characterized by pronounced spatiotemporal variations in thermal gradients. The Western Himalayan syntaxis (WHS) records evidence of deep subduction with low thermal gradients, whereas the Eastern Himalaya is characterized by synchronous high-temperature granulite overprinting. The geodynamic mechanism responsible for these along-strike thermo-metamorphic differences remains poorly constrained, particularly regarding the timing and duration of heating during the India-Asia collision. This study investigates high-pressure pelitic granulites from the Eastern Himalayan syntaxis (EHS) to constrain their metamorphic evolution and duration of metamorphic stages. Pelitic granulites record a clockwise isothermal decompression pressure-temperature (P−T) path with peak conditions of 12−13 kbar and 815−830 °C, accompanied by muscovite-biotite dehydration melting. Metamorphic ages range from 47.3 Ma to 12.6 Ma, with long-lived prograde heating spanning from 47.3 Ma to 23.0 Ma. During this interval, zircon and monazite show increasing Y contents and decreasing (Dy/Yb)N ratios with decreasing age due to the breakdown of accessory minerals (e.g., xenotime and apatite). In contrast, retrograde metamorphism occurs at ca. 23.0−12.6 Ma and shows a reverse trend. The EHS thus documents prolonged (>20 m.y.) prograde heating, contrasting with the rapid subduction-exhumation in the WHS. Integrating the presence of Eocene magmatism, different P−T−time paths, and metamorphic ages between the EHS and WHS, this study proposes that the along-strike metamorphic differences reflect the evolving dynamics of the subduction slab system. The Neo-Tethys breakoff occurs earlier (ca. 47−45 Ma) in the EHS, thus resulting in flat subduction of the Indian lithosphere, Eocene magmatic flare-up, and long-lived prograde heating in the EHS. Conversely, the WHS experienced continuous continental subduction, with Neo-Tethyan breakoff occurring later (ca. 35 Ma) during the transition from subduction to collision. Published: 2026-04-27T18:01:30+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.563 (consider)

The Himalaya is the most prominent collisional belt on Earth, characterized by pronounced spatiotemporal variations in thermal gradients. The Western Himalayan syntaxis (WHS) records evidence of deep subduction with low thermal gradients, whereas the Eastern Himalaya is characterized by synchronous high-temperature granulite overprinting. The geodynamic mechanism responsible for these along-strike thermo-metamorphic differences remains poorly constrained, particularly regarding the timing and duration of heating during the India-Asia collision. This study investigates high-pressure pelitic granulites from the Eastern Himalayan syntaxis (EHS) to constrain their metamorphic evolution and duration of metamorphic stages. Pelitic granulites record a clockwise isothermal decompression pressure-temperature (P−T) path with peak conditions of 12−13 kbar and 815−830 °C, accompanied by muscovite-biotite dehydration melting. During this interval, zircon and monazite show increasing Y contents and decreasing (Dy/Yb)N ratios with decreasing age due to the breakdown of accessory minerals (e.g., xenotime and apatite). In contrast, retrograde metamorphism occurs at ca. 23.0−12.6 Ma and shows a reverse trend. The Neo-Tethys breakoff occurs earlier (ca. 47−45 Ma) in the EHS, thus resulting in flat subduction of the Indian lithosphere, Eocene magmatic flare-up, and long-lived prograde heating in the EHS. Conversely, the WHS experienced continuous continental subduction, with Neo-Tethyan breakoff occurring later (ca. 35 Ma) during the transition from subduction to collision.

item.title: A >300 Myr Long‐Lived Topographic Highland Along the Northern North China Craton Margin Driven by Multistage Continental Convergence
item.dc:creator: Heng Peng
Licheng Wang
Jianqiang Wang
Yazuo Niu
Yahui Yue
Jingbo Sun
Bitian Wei
Chiyang Liu
Massimiliano Zattin
Lin Ding
item.description: Abstract Topographic highlands commonly develop along convergent plate boundaries through long‐term processes such as subduction and continental collision. However, the pre‐Cenozoic mountain‐building history of deep‐time orogenic systems in northeastern Pangaea remains poorly constrained due to later tectonic overprinting and denudation. Here we present detrital zircon U–Pb–Hf data to reconstruct the Phanerozoic provenance and exhumation of the northern North China Craton (NCC). Our results indicate that the Inner Mongolia Paleo‐uplift of northern NCC margin was the primary source of Late Paleozoic–Mesozoic sediments in the region. Integrated with crustal thickness estimates, thermal histories, and paleogeographic reconstructions, our results demonstrate >300 Myr of continuous exhumation along the northern NCC margin, defining it as a long‐lived topographic highland. We attribute this protracted uplift to the successive closure of the Paleo‐Asian and Mongol–Okhotsk oceans, which maintained a convergent tectonic regime in the northeastern Pangaea. Published: 2026-04-24T02:37:28+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.562 (consider)

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl120717

Abstract Topographic highlands commonly develop along convergent plate boundaries through long‐term processes such as subduction and continental collision. However, the pre‐Cenozoic mountain‐building history of deep‐time orogenic systems in northeastern Pangaea remains poorly constrained due to later tectonic overprinting and denudation. Here we present detrital zircon U–Pb–Hf data to reconstruct the Phanerozoic provenance and exhumation of the northern North China Craton (NCC). Integrated with crustal thickness estimates, thermal histories, and paleogeographic reconstructions, our results demonstrate >300 Myr of continuous exhumation along the northern NCC margin, defining it as a long‐lived topographic highland. We attribute this protracted uplift to the successive closure of the Paleo‐Asian and Mongol–Okhotsk oceans, which maintained a convergent tectonic regime in the northeastern Pangaea.

item.title: Hafnium and silicon isotope evidence for multiple unsilicified basaltic sources at the origin of Rhyacian TTG magmas in the Mineiro Belt (Brazil)
item.dc:creator: Syro Lacerda
Martin Guitreau
Leonardo Gonçalves
Hugo Moreira
Cristiane C. Gonçalves
Stéphany Rodrigues Lopes
Sandro Mauri
Marco Aurélio Pinheiro
Cassiano Costa e Castro
Régis Doucelance
item.description: Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTGs) are silica-rich plutonic rocks that correspond to the dominant lithologies of Archean basements. Understanding their formation is essential to unravel the early Earth tectonic regime and constrain continental crust formation and evolution. Archean TTGs commonly exhibit heavy-silicon isotope compositions attributed to ocean-derived silica in their sources, thereby indicating an interaction with the surface. The sedimentary record shows that the dissolved silica in seawater (DSi) has decreased secularly from the Archean onward. Despite the general disappearance of TTGs after the Archean, some granitoids of TTG affinity can still be found in Paleoproterozoic crustal domains that are tectonically well-constrained. Comparing the geochemical characteristics of Archean and Proterozoic TTGs, as well as their sources, therefore appears important for feeding debates on the evolution of continental crust and tectonic settings. To shed new light on these questions, the present study examines the magmatic record of three Paleoproterozoic TTG units in the Mineiro Belt (Brazil) by focusing on whole-rock petrography and geochemistry alongside zircon U-Pb geochronology, elemental, and isotopic (Hf and Si) geochemistry. These new data indicate episodic formation of TTG magmas during the Rhyacian (2.15–2.10 Ga), mainly driven by melting of Rb- and Ba-enriched low-K mafic sources with variably long crustal residence times (∼0.2–0.8 Ga). Studied Paleoproterozoic TTGs exhibit lower silicon isotopic signatures than their Archean counterparts. This difference is likely linked to variations in source pre-melting histories, such that they may not have been hydrothermally silicified, seawater may have had a distinct Si isotope signature, or the mechanisms of Si enrichment may not have been the same as for Archean TTG sources. Finally, a limited, though detectable, influence of pre-existing crust is nevertheless visible in these magmas through zircon inheritance, which could also account for some of the low Si isotope signatures observed. Our study also provides an alternative explanation for the apparent homogenization of Si isotope signatures in the upper-continental crust over time, since Proterozoic TTGs can exhibit relatively low Si isotope signatures compared to their Archean equivalents. Published: 2026-04-26T22:21:34+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.560 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Syro Lacerda; Martin Guitreau; Leonardo Gonçalves; Hugo Moreira; Cristiane C. Gonçalves; Stéphany Rodrigues Lopes; Sandro Mauri; Marco Aurélio Pinheiro; Cassiano Costa e Castro; Régis Doucelance

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.026

Relevance: 0.560 (consider)

Tonalite-trondhjemite-granodiorite (TTGs) are silica-rich plutonic rocks that correspond to the dominant lithologies of Archean basements. Understanding their formation is essential to unravel the early Earth tectonic regime and constrain continental crust formation and evolution. Archean TTGs commonly exhibit heavy-silicon isotope compositions attributed to ocean-derived silica in their sources, thereby indicating an interaction with the surface. The sedimentary record shows that the dissolved silica in seawater (DSi) has decreased secularly from the Archean onward. Despite the general disappearance of TTGs after the Archean, some granitoids of TTG affinity can still be found in Paleoproterozoic crustal domains that are tectonically well-constrained. Comparing the geochemical characteristics of Archean and Proterozoic TTGs, as well as their sources, therefore appears important for feeding debates on the evolution of continental crust and tectonic settings. To shed new light on these questions, the present study examines the magmatic record of three Paleoproterozoic TTG units in the Mineiro Belt (Brazil) by focusing on whole-rock petrography and geochemistry alongside zircon U-Pb geochronology, elemental, and isotopic (Hf and Si) geochemistry. These new data indicate episodic formation of TTG magmas during the Rhyacian (2.15–2.10 Ga), mainly driven by melting of Rb- and Ba-enriched low-K mafic sources with variably long crustal residence times (∼0.2–0.8 Ga). Studied Paleoproterozoic TTGs exhibit lower silicon isotopic signatures than their Archean counterparts. This difference is likely linked to variations in source pre-melting histories, such that they may not have been hydrothermally silicified, seawater may have had a distinct Si isotope signature, or the mechanisms of Si enrichment may not have been the same as for Archean TTG sources. Finally, a limited, though detectable, influence of pre-existing crust is nevertheless visible in these magmas through zircon inheritance, which could also account for some of the low Si isotope signatures observed. Our study also provides an alternative explanation for the apparent homogenization of Si isotope signatures in the upper-continental crust over time, since Proterozoic TTGs can exhibit relatively low Si isotope signatures compared to their Archean equivalents.

item.title: Persistent geochemical zonation (“striping”) within the Galápagos mantle plume
item.dc:creator: Matthew Gleeson
Mark Richards
Cinzia G. Farnetani
Kaj Hoernle
Sally Gibson
item.description: Some hotspot tracks, such as those formed by the Hawai’i and Galápagos mantle plumes, exhibit long-lived cross-track isotopic zonation, thought to reflect the streaking out of heterogeneous material in the plume conduit during upwelling. In lavas associated with the Galápagos mantle plume, three geochemical domains, present for at least 15 Myr, have been identified: northern, southern and central. The most extreme isotopic enrichments are observed in the northern domain of the Cocos Ridge at ∼15 Ma, and in the southern domain of the Galápagos Archipelago at the present day. Owing to the northward migration of the Galápagos Spreading Centre above the plume since ∼20 Ma, this relationship suggests that geochemical enrichment in the Galápagos basalts is always greatest above the region of the plume furthest from the nearby mid-ocean ridge. Here we demonstrate that the temporal variations in geochemical enrichment associated with Galápagos plume stripes likely reflect ''shallow mantle control'' associated with differences in the mean depth of melting. We conduct forward melting models of a mixed peridotite-pyroxenite mantle to calculate the isotopic composition of the resulting melts formed under two mantle flow regimes. Our results demonstrate that variations in the average pressure of melt generation, due to the influence of the nearby ridge axis, may explain the range of isotopic compositions across ∼15 Ma of Galápagos plume-related volcanism. The patterns of isotopic zonation observed along the hotspot track strongly confirm the paradigm of persistent plume striping, with variations in the degree of geochemical enrichment modulated by shallow mantle processes. Published: 2026-04-23T03:42:11+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.560 (consider)

Some hotspot tracks, such as those formed by the Hawai’i and Galápagos mantle plumes, exhibit long-lived cross-track isotopic zonation, thought to reflect the streaking out of heterogeneous material in the plume conduit during upwelling. In lavas associated with the Galápagos mantle plume, three geochemical domains, present for at least 15 Myr, have been identified: northern, southern and central. The most extreme isotopic enrichments are observed in the northern domain of the Cocos Ridge at ∼15 Ma, and in the southern domain of the Galápagos Archipelago at the present day. Owing to the northward migration of the Galápagos Spreading Centre above the plume since ∼20 Ma, this relationship suggests that geochemical enrichment in the Galápagos basalts is always greatest above the region of the plume furthest from the nearby mid-ocean ridge. Here we demonstrate that the temporal variations in geochemical enrichment associated with Galápagos plume stripes likely reflect ''shallow mantle control'' associated with differences in the mean depth of melting. Our results demonstrate that variations in the average pressure of melt generation, due to the influence of the nearby ridge axis, may explain the range of isotopic compositions across ∼15 Ma of Galápagos plume-related volcanism. The patterns of isotopic zonation observed along the hotspot track strongly confirm the paradigm of persistent plume striping, with variations in the degree of geochemical enrichment modulated by shallow mantle processes.

item.title: Viscosity and structure of hydrous silicate liquids: Constraints on oceanic hydrothermal circulation
item.dc:creator: Qi Chen
Craig Lundstrom
Yanchong Li
Young Jay Ryu
Tony Yu
Stella Chariton
Dongzhou Zhang
Vitali Prakapenka
Yanbin Wang
item.description: Despite its central role in cooling the oceanic lithosphere, the efficiency and geometry of hydrothermal circulation remain debated. While ridge-scale simulations typically adopt pure-water transport properties, circulating liquids at the magmatic-hydrothermal transition can dissolve appreciable silicate at elevated temperatures and pressures. These silicate-bearing liquids likely mediate heat and mass transfer, yet their mobility is poorly constrained. Here we present viscosity measurements of hydrous Na 2 Si 2 O 5 liquids (HNS2) at 130–250 °C using in-situ falling-sphere viscometry in a hydrothermal diamond-anvil cell and characterize liquid structure with Raman spectroscopy up to 350 °C and 440 MPa. Measured HNS2 viscosities increase by ∼one order of magnitude (7.35 × 10 –4 to 9.60 × 10 –3 Pa·s) as Na 2 Si 2 O 5 increases from 25.4 to 69.7 wt.%. Raman spectra indicate systematic shifts toward less connected Si-O environments with increasing H 2 O and temperature, consistent with the lower viscosity in H 2 O-rich liquids. Incorporating these data into two-dimensional porous-flow simulations of hydrothermal circulation shows that compared to pure water, introducing modest dissolved silicate weakens distal off-axis circulation while preserving a ridge-proximal primary cell that reaches the brittle-ductile transition depth. This suggests that pure-water models are likely to overestimate the lateral extent of off-axis advection required to match conductive backgrounds. More generally, silicate-bearing liquids can remain sufficiently mobile to contribute to lower-crustal heat extraction and may help account for pervasive low-grade (greenschist-facies) hydrothermal alteration in gabbroic and sheeted-dike sections. Silicate contents in deep liquids thus should be incorporated into quantitative models of crustal cooling. Published: 2026-04-23T22:26:57+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.559 (ignore)

Authors: Qi Chen; Craig Lundstrom; Yanchong Li; Young Jay Ryu; Tony Yu; Stella Chariton; Dongzhou Zhang; Vitali Prakapenka; Yanbin Wang

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120059

Relevance: 0.559 (ignore)

Despite its central role in cooling the oceanic lithosphere, the efficiency and geometry of hydrothermal circulation remain debated. While ridge-scale simulations typically adopt pure-water transport properties, circulating liquids at the magmatic-hydrothermal transition can dissolve appreciable silicate at elevated temperatures and pressures. These silicate-bearing liquids likely mediate heat and mass transfer, yet their mobility is poorly constrained. Here we present viscosity measurements of hydrous Na 2 Si 2 O 5 liquids (HNS2) at 130–250 °C using in-situ falling-sphere viscometry in a hydrothermal diamond-anvil cell and characterize liquid structure with Raman spectroscopy up to 350 °C and 440 MPa. Measured HNS2 viscosities increase by ∼one order of magnitude (7.35 × 10 –4 to 9.60 × 10 –3 Pa·s) as Na 2 Si 2 O 5 increases from 25.4 to 69.7 wt.%. Raman spectra indicate systematic shifts toward less connected Si-O environments with increasing H 2 O and temperature, consistent with the lower viscosity in H 2 O-rich liquids. Incorporating these data into two-dimensional porous-flow simulations of hydrothermal circulation shows that compared to pure water, introducing modest dissolved silicate weakens distal off-axis circulation while preserving a ridge-proximal primary cell that reaches the brittle-ductile transition depth. This suggests that pure-water models are likely to overestimate the lateral extent of off-axis advection required to match conductive backgrounds. More generally, silicate-bearing liquids can remain sufficiently mobile to contribute to lower-crustal heat extraction and may help account for pervasive low-grade (greenschist-facies) hydrothermal alteration in gabbroic and sheeted-dike sections. Silicate contents in deep liquids thus should be incorporated into quantitative models of crustal cooling.

item.title: A Common Mantle Source for the Endogenous CO <sub>2</sub> Degassed at the Eifel (Western Germany) and the Ardennes (Eastern Belgium) Regions
item.dc:creator: Lisa Ricci
Francesco Frondini
Daniele Morgavi
Stefano Caliro
Andrea Luca Rizzo
Tobias P. Fischer
Giovanni Chiodini
item.description: Abstract Tectonic CO 2 Earth degassing is globally relevant and has probably controlled climate on a geological scale (Brune et al., 2019, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41561‐017‐0003‐6 ). Endogenous CO 2 outgassing from rifting areas remains poorly constrained, with most available data from the East African Rift. Here, we investigate the CO 2 degassing in the European Cenozoic Rift System (ECRIS), focusing on the chemical and isotopic composition of 161 springs emerging from the Eifel‐Ardennes‐Rhenish Massif region (Eastern Belgium‐Western Germany). Theoretical water‐gas‐rock interaction models based on the chemistry of gases dissolved in East Eifel groundwaters reveal CO 2 efflux takes place in a P ‐ T range between the aquifer depth (4 bar, 160°C) and spring emergence (1 bar, 9°C). He and C isotopes show that Ardennes, Volcanic Eifel and Rhenish Massif are all part of a unique degassing system. The average mantle CO 2 flux emitted from the region is 5 ± 2 × 10 6 mol yr −1 km −2 , corresponding to a total deeply derived CO 2 emission rate of 7 ± 4 × 10 9 mol yr −1 . These values are of the same magnitude as the global baseline defined for convective hydrothermal CO 2 emitted from areas of high heat flow, demonstrating the relevance of passive rifts as CO 2 emitters on a global scale. Published: 2026-04-26T07:39:56+00:00 Venue: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems Score: 0.556 (ignore)

Journal: Geochemistry, Geophysics, Geosystems

DOI: 10.1029/2025gc012735

Relevance: 0.556 (ignore)

Abstract Tectonic CO 2 Earth degassing is globally relevant and has probably controlled climate on a geological scale (Brune et al., 2019, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41561‐017‐0003‐6 ). Endogenous CO 2 outgassing from rifting areas remains poorly constrained, with most available data from the East African Rift. Here, we investigate the CO 2 degassing in the European Cenozoic Rift System (ECRIS), focusing on the chemical and isotopic composition of 161 springs emerging from the Eifel‐Ardennes‐Rhenish Massif region (Eastern Belgium‐Western Germany). Theoretical water‐gas‐rock interaction models based on the chemistry of gases dissolved in East Eifel groundwaters reveal CO 2 efflux takes place in a P ‐ T range between the aquifer depth (4 bar, 160°C) and spring emergence (1 bar, 9°C). He and C isotopes show that Ardennes, Volcanic Eifel and Rhenish Massif are all part of a unique degassing system. The average mantle CO 2 flux emitted from the region is 5 ± 2 × 10 6 mol yr −1 km −2 , corresponding to a total deeply derived CO 2 emission rate of 7 ± 4 × 10 9 mol yr −1 . These values are of the same magnitude as the global baseline defined for convective hydrothermal CO 2 emitted from areas of high heat flow, demonstrating the relevance of passive rifts as CO 2 emitters on a global scale.

item.title: Body-wave anisotropic model AniCD-L: Illuminating the zonation of lithospheric azimuthal anisotropy by XKS splitting in the Sichuan-Yunnan region of SW China
item.dc:creator: Xinyi Li
Yuan Gao
Yutao Shi
Yiwen Yang
item.description: The Sichuan-Yunnan region (SYR) in the eastern and southeastern margins of the Tibetan Plateau covers the central and southern parts of the North-South Seismic Zone in China. As a key frontier tectonic zone of the India–Eurasia collision, its lithospheric deformation mechanisms are crucial for understanding plateau material extrusion and intracontinental tectonic evolution. In this study, we present a lithospheric azimuthal anisotropic model as a part of the seismic body-wave sphere-layered anisotropic model for the SYR. The lithospheric anisotropy parameters are derived from teleseismic XKS (including SKS, PKS, and SKKS phases) splitting. The model reveals a pronounced north-south contrast in lithospheric anisotropy across the SYR. In the north, anisotropy is oriented predominantly NW–SE, whereas in the south, it rotates to an approximately E–W orientation, with a sudden change near 26.5°N. This boundary reflects differences in upper mantle dynamic processes. In the north, the model supports the crust-mantle coupling, reflecting likely coherent eastward extrusion of Tibetan Plateau crust and mantle material under the ongoing northward subduction of the Indian plate. In the south, the model supports the crust-mantle decoupling, associated with likely slab rollback-induced mantle flow. The spatial distribution shows clear zonation in deep deformation patterns, closely related to tectonic blocks. In addition, in several small local zones, the inconsistencies among XKS results from different studies indicate the presence of complex deep structures, which require further constraints with better measurement data. Published: 2026-04-28T03:31:44+00:00 Venue: Science China Earth Sciences Score: 0.551 (ignore)

The Sichuan-Yunnan region (SYR) in the eastern and southeastern margins of the Tibetan Plateau covers the central and southern parts of the North-South Seismic Zone in China. As a key frontier tectonic zone of the India–Eurasia collision, its lithospheric deformation mechanisms are crucial for understanding plateau material extrusion and intracontinental tectonic evolution. In this study, we present a lithospheric azimuthal anisotropic model as a part of the seismic body-wave sphere-layered anisotropic model for the SYR. The lithospheric anisotropy parameters are derived from teleseismic XKS (including SKS, PKS, and SKKS phases) splitting. The model reveals a pronounced north-south contrast in lithospheric anisotropy across the SYR. In the north, anisotropy is oriented predominantly NW–SE, whereas in the south, it rotates to an approximately E–W orientation, with a sudden change near 26.5°N. This boundary reflects differences in upper mantle dynamic processes. In the north, the model supports the crust-mantle coupling, reflecting likely coherent eastward extrusion of Tibetan Plateau crust and mantle material under the ongoing northward subduction of the Indian plate. In the south, the model supports the crust-mantle decoupling, associated with likely slab rollback-induced mantle flow. The spatial distribution shows clear zonation in deep deformation patterns, closely related to tectonic blocks. In addition, in several small local zones, the inconsistencies among XKS results from different studies indicate the presence of complex deep structures, which require further constraints with better measurement data.

item.title: Fault-mediated magma propagation and triggered seismicity revealed by the 2022 São Jorge Azores unrest
item.dc:creator: Stephen P. Hicks
Pablo J. Gonzalez
Anthony Lomax
Ana M. G. Ferreira
Ricardo S. Ramalho
Neil C. Mitchell
Graça Silveira
Nuno Afonso Dias
João Fontiela
Rui Fernandes
Susana Custódio
Maria Tsekhmistrenko
Virgilio Mendes
Adriano Pimentel
Rita Silva
Gonçalo Prates
William Sturgeon
Augustin Marignier
Fernando Carrilho
Rui Marques
Miguel Miranda
Arturo M. Garcia
item.description: Abstract Understanding failed volcanic eruptions is key to mapping magma plumbing and forecasting hazards. Faults and fractures guide magma, but their mechanisms remain unclear due to the lack of precise earthquake locations and limited 3-D fault mapping in volcanic regions. The triple-junction setting of the Azores Archipelago, where volcanic systems and seismogenic faults coexist, offers a natural laboratory to study fault–magma interactions. We analysed ~18,000 earthquakes relocated to high precision using onshore and ocean-bottom seismometers, combined with geodetic data and seismic autocorrelation imaging, during a failed 2022 eruption on São Jorge Island. A magmatic dike ascended rapidly and mostly aseismically from the upper mantle, intruding a crustal fault before stalling ~1,600 m below the surface. Seismicity indicates that magma branching and lateral fluid escape along the fault triggered an intense, months-long swarm with rotated focal mechanisms. This study demonstrates the dual role of faults in facilitating and arresting magma ascent. Published: 2026-04-23T09:02:44+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.550 (ignore)

Garcia

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-71668-6

Relevance: 0.550 (ignore)

Abstract Understanding failed volcanic eruptions is key to mapping magma plumbing and forecasting hazards. Faults and fractures guide magma, but their mechanisms remain unclear due to the lack of precise earthquake locations and limited 3-D fault mapping in volcanic regions. The triple-junction setting of the Azores Archipelago, where volcanic systems and seismogenic faults coexist, offers a natural laboratory to study fault–magma interactions. We analysed ~18,000 earthquakes relocated to high precision using onshore and ocean-bottom seismometers, combined with geodetic data and seismic autocorrelation imaging, during a failed 2022 eruption on São Jorge Island. A magmatic dike ascended rapidly and mostly aseismically from the upper mantle, intruding a crustal fault before stalling ~1,600 m below the surface. Seismicity indicates that magma branching and lateral fluid escape along the fault triggered an intense, months-long swarm with rotated focal mechanisms. This study demonstrates the dual role of faults in facilitating and arresting magma ascent.

item.title: Rheological contrast traps and controls MW 6.8 Michilla (northern Chile) intraslab aftershocks
item.dc:creator: Francisco Pastén-Araya
Sebastián Carrasco
Catalina Morales-Yáñez
Efraín Rivera
Leoncio Cabrera
Sergio Ruiz
Pablo Salazar
item.description: The factors controlling the extent and distribution of aftershocks in intermediate-depth earthquakes remain poorly understood, largely due to the scarcity of dense seismological networks and high-resolution seismic catalogs. Between 2013 and 2015 CE, a temporary dense seismic network installed in northern Chile allowed the detailed monitoring of a peculiar, long-lasting aftershock sequence confined within the rupture zone of the 16 December 2007 MW 6.8 Michilla intermediate-depth earthquake, located within the subducting oceanic plate. This unique dataset allowed us to compile a high-resolution seismic catalog and to analyze the possible factors that influence this type of rupture. Our results show that seismicity was trapped between the upper and lower oceanic crust, correlating strongly with expected dehydration reactions in basaltic and gabbroic rheologies. In addition, aftershocks were distributed across a complex multi-fault system, which can remain active for an extended period through the same dehydration process. These observations provide new constraints on the mechanics of intermediate-depth rupture and highlight the role of dehydration-driven processes in controlling their seismic behavior, with implications for seismic hazard in subduction zones. Published: 2026-04-24T19:30:48+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.547 (ignore)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Francisco Pastén-Araya; Sebastián Carrasco; Catalina Morales-Yáñez; Efraín Rivera; Leoncio Cabrera; Sergio Ruiz; Pablo Salazar

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g53833.1

Relevance: 0.547 (ignore)

The factors controlling the extent and distribution of aftershocks in intermediate-depth earthquakes remain poorly understood, largely due to the scarcity of dense seismological networks and high-resolution seismic catalogs. Between 2013 and 2015 CE, a temporary dense seismic network installed in northern Chile allowed the detailed monitoring of a peculiar, long-lasting aftershock sequence confined within the rupture zone of the 16 December 2007 MW 6.8 Michilla intermediate-depth earthquake, located within the subducting oceanic plate. This unique dataset allowed us to compile a high-resolution seismic catalog and to analyze the possible factors that influence this type of rupture. Our results show that seismicity was trapped between the upper and lower oceanic crust, correlating strongly with expected dehydration reactions in basaltic and gabbroic rheologies. In addition, aftershocks were distributed across a complex multi-fault system, which can remain active for an extended period through the same dehydration process. These observations provide new constraints on the mechanics of intermediate-depth rupture and highlight the role of dehydration-driven processes in controlling their seismic behavior, with implications for seismic hazard in subduction zones.
